title: Advance directives at admission
vignette: A 79-year-old man is undergoing evaluation for hospital admission due to an exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. His wife expresses concern about his health due to multiple hospitalizations over the last year for various complications related to his comorbidities. The patient has smoked cigarettes daily for more than 50 years. Medical history includes coronary artery disease, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and obesity. After performing a thorough history and physical examination, the physician prepares to admit the patient and decides to continue his existing medications for diabetes and heart disease. Admission orders also include supplemental oxygen, bronchodilators, antibiotics, and systemic corticosteroids. Which of the following additional components is most critical to include during the admission process?

clues.text: exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
clues.note: A serious, potentially life-threatening illness prompting admission. The severity is what makes clarifying goals of care time-sensitive.

clues.text: multiple hospitalizations over the last year
clues.note: Recurrent admissions signal advanced, progressive disease with real risk of deterioration. This is exactly the patient for whom code status / advance directives must be established.

clues.text: coronary artery disease, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and obesity
clues.note: A heavy comorbidity burden compounds his risk of acute decompensation, reinforcing the need to clarify his wishes up front rather than during a crisis.

whatMatters: The medical management (oxygen, bronchodilators, steroids, antibiotics) is already ordered - the question asks what ELSE is most critical AT ADMISSION. For a seriously ill patient who could deteriorate unexpectedly, establishing advance directives / code status (and a health care proxy) is the required, time-sensitive step. Smoking cessation, diet, social work, and insurance matter but are not admission-critical. Answer B.
strategy: When a sick patient is being ADMITTED and the options mix 'good medical advice' (smoking cessation, diet) with 'advance directives / code status,' choose the time-sensitive task: documenting advance directives and resuscitation preferences - because acute deterioration can happen at any time. Lifestyle counseling and logistics belong to the hospital course or discharge.

options.text: Discussion about diet and exercise to improve glycemic control and heart health
Discussion of whether the patient has advance directives or a health care proxy
Discussion regarding smoking cessation to reduce the patient's risk of dying from heart or lung disease
Facilitation of a social work assessment to arrange for home health care and expedite discharge
Verification of insurance coverage to determine eligibility for supplemental oxygen at home

eliminations.label: diet/exercise counseling - not admission-critical
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: lifestyle counseling for diabetes and coronary disease - appropriate preventive care. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it's not time-sensitive and can be addressed during the hospital course or at discharge, not specifically 'during admission.' ✅ Correct if: a clinic visit focused on long-term risk-factor modification.

eliminations.label: smoking cessation - best long-term, but not most critical now (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: smoking cessation is the single most effective long-term step to reduce his cardiopulmonary mortality. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it's hugely important but NOT what's most critical to complete during admission; code status/advance directives are time-sensitive because he could deteriorate at any moment. The trap is picking the 'most medically beneficial' over the 'most critical at admission.' ✅ Correct if: asked for the best intervention to reduce future morbidity/mortality.

eliminations.label: social work / home health - a discharge task
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: arranging home health care is part of discharge planning. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it belongs later in the hospital course, not the critical admission step. ✅ Correct if: planning a safe discharge.

eliminations.label: insurance verification - logistics, not clinical priority
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: an administrative eligibility check. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it isn't a clinical priority and isn't what must occur during admission. ✅ Correct if: a billing/logistics question.

reasoning: A seriously ill patient with advanced COPD + multiple comorbidities is being admitted
Acute deterioration can occur unexpectedly, so his wishes must be known in advance
All admitted patients should be asked about resuscitation preferences and advance directives / health care proxy
This is the time-sensitive admission-critical step -> answer B
mechanism: Advance directives record a patient's care preferences if they lose decision-making capacity; they include a living will (specific end-of-life wishes) and a health care proxy (a surrogate decision-maker who applies substituted judgment - deciding what the patient would have wanted). On hospital admission, all patients should be asked about resuscitation preferences (e.g., full code vs DNR) and whether advance directives exist, because serious clinical deterioration can occur unexpectedly. For a patient with severe COPD and multiple comorbidities this is especially pressing. The other items (smoking cessation, diet, social work, insurance) are appropriate during the hospital course or at discharge but are not the critical admission step.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Ethics > advance directives: living will vs health care proxy/durable power of attorney; substituted judgment; code status discussion required at admission.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Notice the qualifier 'most critical to include DURING THE ADMISSION PROCESS.' That favors the time-sensitive ethics step (advance directives/code status) over generally-good counseling.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on advance directive, living will, health care proxy, durable power of attorney, substituted judgment, code status/DNR? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Smoking cessation (C) is the trap - it's the most medically valuable long-term advice, so it 'feels' right. But the admission-critical task is establishing code status / advance directives.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank: at admission of a seriously ill patient, clarify code status / advance directives first - deterioration can be sudden.

keywords.front: Most critical to address at hospital admission of a seriously ill patient
Health care proxy / durable power of attorney

keywords.back: Decide what the patient would have wanted

keywords.front: Best long-term intervention in a COPD/CAD smoker

firstAid.detail: Ethics > living will vs health care proxy; ask all admitted patients about code status because deterioration can be unexpected.
firstAid.location: Ethics > living will vs health care proxy; ask all admitted patients about code status because deterioration can be unexpected.

firstAid.detail: Ethics/Prevention > smoking cessation and lifestyle counseling (valuable but not admission-critical); discharge planning and insurance (logistics).
firstAid.location: Ethics/Prevention > smoking cessation and lifestyle counseling (valuable but not admission-critical); discharge planning and insurance (logistics).

firstAid.detail: Pulm > bronchodilators, systemic steroids, antibiotics, controlled O2 (already ordered here).
firstAid.location: Pulm > bronchodilators, systemic steroids, antibiotics, controlled O2 (already ordered here).

memoryHook: Sick patient hitting the hospital door -> clarify code status / advance directives FIRST; he could crash before the smoking-cessation talk ever matters.
